description: Static-target A/B scenario. Single goto + wait_stable on iana.org example-domains. Deterministic page (RFC 2606 stable, no JS deps, no auth, no rate limit). Same actions/capture for control + treatment; only capture.act_receipt differs.
rationale_target_choice: iana.org example-domains is chosen because it has NO meaningful DOM jitter — the page is byte-stable across calls. Used as a baseline: if a generic strip is already perfect on this target, act_receipt cannot outperform it (the delta is expected to be 0pp). The interesting result on a static target is the invariant: byte-stability of canonical hashing across runtimes (JS, Python, Camoufox-driven browser MCP).

control.description: Baseline op:'act'-style behavior — no receipt emitted.

treatment.description: New act_receipt.v1 behavior — handler emits structured receipt with jitter-stripped canonical bytes.

expected_outcome.control_cache_friendly_score: ≥ 0.80 (generic strip is highly effective on stable targets like iana.org)
expected_outcome.treatment_cache_friendly_score: ≥ control (act_receipt strips MORE jitter than the raw response — observability subtree fully removed, dom_region_size_bytes stripped). If treatment < control, the receipt assembly has a bug.
expected_outcome.delta_interpretation: Positive delta = act_receipt achieves what it was designed to achieve (more cache-friendly than the raw response). Zero delta on a static target is the expected null result. Negative delta = receipt is leaking jitter not yet stripped.
